raw_case:
Incidental aortic valve papillary fibroelastoma diagnosed by transesophageal echocardiography in a patient undergoing coronary artery bypass surgery: a case report


Abstract

Papillary fibroelastoma of aortic valve is a rare benign tumor that can present with symptoms of obstruction or embolization and can be asymptomatic. The main stay of diagnosis is echocardiography. The size of the tumor affects the sensitivity of transthoracic echocardiography which can miss small size tumors. The treatment is surgical resection. Here, we report a case of papillary fibroelastoma of aortic valve that was detected intraoperatively by transesophageal echocardiography and was missed by transthoracic echocardiography in a patient undergoing coronary artery surgery. The tumor was resected successfully with preservation of aortic valve with no complication. This case highlights importance of intraoperative transesophageal echocardiography in making the diagnosis.

Introduction

Papillary fibroelastoma (PFE) is a rare benign tumor that can vary in size and symptomatology. The mainstay of diagnosis is echocardiography with higher sensitivity of transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) over transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) in tumor detection. Here, we report a rare case of PFE of aortic valve in a patient scheduled to undergo coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery where TEE made the diagnosis that was missed by preoperative TTE.

Case report

A 55-year-old man with hypertension and hyperlipidemia presented to our hospital with non-ST-segment myocardial infarction (NSTEMI). He was a smoker with no family history of heart disease. Patient was having class III angina for the previous 6 months but never sought medical help. He had no shortness of breath and no history of previous cerebrovascular accident (CVA). The patient underwent coronary angiogram that showed triple vessel disease with chronically occluded right coronary artery. Preoperative TTE showed left ventricular ejection fraction of 50% with mild mitral valve regurgitation and no aortic valve disease (Fig. 1A). Chest radiography was normal and his preoperative blood tests including C-reactive protein, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and complete blood count were normal. Blood cultures were negative. Patient underwent CABG during the same hospitalization due to his recurrent chest pain. Intraoperative TEE (Fig. 1B) showed a mass in the undersurface (ventricular side) of the non-coronary cusp (NCC) of aortic valve with normal valve function (tri leaflet valve). The differential diagnosis at that time included vegetation, calcification, or tumor. Median sternotomy was performed with ascending aorta and right atrial venous cannulation. CABG was performed with left internal mammary artery anastomosis to left anterior descending artery and reverse saphenous vein graft anastomosis to obtuse marginal artery. The aortic valve was exposed through transverse aortotomy and the mass was visualized in the ventricular side of NCC of aortic valve measuring 6 × 5 mm. It was white in color, rounded with some fronds (Fig. 2). The aortic valve was tri-leaflet valve with some calcifications noted in the other two cusps. The mass was then shaved off the valve and sent to histopathology. The leaflet was intact, and the aortic valve was tested and was normal with no regurgitation. Patient tolerated the procedure well and was weaned from cardiopulmonary bypass with small dose of Dobutamine. TEE at the end of operation showed well-functioning aortic valve with no aortic regurgitation. Patient was extubated 7 hours postoperatively and he had uncomplicated postoperative recovery and was discharged home 1 week postoperatively on dual antiplatelet therapy (Aspirin and Clopidegrol). Pathology report of the aortic mass (Fig. 3) showed 0.6 × 0.5 × 0.4 cm PFE (microscopic section showed multiple branching fronds and paucicellular avascular fibro elastic tissue lined by a single layer of endocardium). Patient remained well and asymptomatic at 3 months postoperatively.

Discussion

PFEs are rare and slow-growing avascular benign tumors with sizes ranging from 2 to 70 mm. They are classically characterized by multiple papillary fronds which give a sea anemone-like appearance. Histologically they are comprised of a single layer of endocardial cells covering the papillary surface, matrix consisting of longitudinally oriented collagen with irregular elastic fibers, proteoglycans and spindle cells that resemble smooth muscle cells or fibroblasts [1]. Commonest location reported is in aortic valve (involving right or non-coronary cusps) in 44.5% of cases and more commonly are on the superior surface (aortic side) than inferior surface of aortic valve (ventricular side) [2]. Majority are single tumors and 44% of them are mobile with a stalk [2]. They are predominantly diagnosed in men at the age of 60 years old with half of patients being asymptomatic. Symptoms reported include dyspnea, CVA, angina, and syncope [2–5]. TTE has a diagnostic accuracy of 85% and classically PFE are small <15 mm round echo-dense and pedunculated mass with high independent motion. Its characteristic shimmery appearance correlated with filiform projections. However, detection of small PFE < 5 mm requires higher resolution than that offered by TTE (as in our case which was missed by TTE and picked up by TEE) [1]. TEE is reported to have higher sensitivity for tumors <2 mm than TTE (77 vs 62%, respectively) [2]. The reasons why TTE may fail to diagnose tumors include the following: when tumor was masked by an associated lesion, too small to be seen, examination was not done carefully with sufficient index of suspicion, and if there were no significant characteristics to differentiate tumor from the degenerative valve disease [2]. Surgical resection remains the treatment of choice in all symptomatic patients or asymptomatic patients if tumor size >1 cm or has increased mobility or undergoing another cardiac procedure as in our case.

Conclusion

The use of intraoperative TEE is of a paramount importance in detection of PFE in patients undergoing cardiac surgery especially in small size tumors that can be otherwise missed by preoperative TTE.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 55-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Recurrent chest pain with history of non-ST-segment myocardial infarction (NSTEMI)
- **History of Present Illness:** Class III angina for 6 months, no shortness of breath, no cerebrovascular accident (CVA) history.
- **Past Medical History:** Hypertension, hyperlipidemia
- **Personal History:** Smoker, no family history of heart disease
- **Family History:** None
- **Physical Examination:** Normal preoperative findings
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Preoperative transthoracic echocardiography (TTE): Left ventricular ejection fraction of 50%, mild mitral valve regurgitation, no aortic valve disease.
  - Coronary angiogram: Triple vessel disease with occluded right coronary artery.
  - Routine bloodwork: Normal (e.g., no elevation in C-reactive protein, ESR, or white blood cell count), blood cultures were negative.
  - Intraoperative transesophageal echocardiography (TEE): Identified a mobile mass (6 × 5 mm) on the ventricular side of the non-coronary cusp (NCC) of the aortic valve with no functional compromise.
  - Histopathology of resected mass: Confirmed papillary fibroelastoma (PFE) characterized by papillary fronds and paucicellular fibroelastic tissue.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Suspicions:**
   - The intraoperative discovery of a mass on the aortic valve during TEE prompted consideration of:
     - **Vegetation**: Ruled out due to absence of infection signs (e.g., negative blood cultures, lack of elevated inflammatory markers like CRP or ESR).
     - **Calcification**: Less likely due to the mass's mobile and soft nature with papillary fronds, unlike the hard, fixed appearance of calcified material.
     - **Tumor (Papillary Fibroelastoma)**: Supported by the mobile, pedunculated nature of the mass, its location on the NCC, and absence of symptoms like obstruction or predisposing conditions. 

2. **Histopathology Confirmation:**
   - The postoperative pathology confirmed the clinical suspicion of a papillary fibroelastoma based on its characteristic microscopic appearance, ruling out alternative diagnoses.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with **papillary fibroelastoma of the aortic valve**, detected by intraoperative TEE and confirmed by histopathology. The tumor was resected successfully without complications. Key factors leading to the diagnosis included TEE visualization of the mobile frond-like mass and the histopathological findings of fibroelastic tissue.

---
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Papillary fibroelastoma of the aortic valve

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient is a 55-year-old male presenting with recurrent chest pain (Class III angina) and has a history of NSTEMI. This immediately suggests ongoing cardiac ischemia and underlying coronary artery disease as the primary concern. The absence of shortness of breath and CVA history helps to narrow down the focus, but angina is the dominant symptom.

<step 2> **Evaluate Past Medical History and Risk Factors:** The patient has hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and is a smoker. These are significant risk factors for coronary artery disease and further support the suspicion of underlying cardiovascular pathology as the cause of his angina and previous NSTEMI.  The lack of family history of heart disease is less relevant given the strong personal risk factors.

<step 3> **Assess Preoperative Investigations:**
    * **TTE:**  Reduced LVEF (50%) indicates some degree of left ventricular dysfunction, likely secondary to ischemic heart disease from the NSTEMI and ongoing coronary artery disease. Mild mitral regurgitation is also noted, which can be associated with ischemic heart disease. No aortic valve disease was initially detected by TTE.
    * **Coronary Angiogram:** This is a critical finding. Triple vessel disease with an occluded RCA confirms severe coronary artery disease and is the most likely cause of the patient's angina and NSTEMI. This explains the chief complaint and history perfectly.
    * **Routine Bloodwork:** Normal bloodwork and negative blood cultures essentially rule out acute infection or inflammatory processes as the primary cause of the chest pain or cardiac issues.  The lack of elevated inflammatory markers further points towards chronic, rather than acute inflammatory, cardiac disease.

<step 4> **Analyze Intraoperative Findings:**
    * **TEE:**  The intraoperative TEE reveals a mobile mass on the aortic valve (NCC). This is a new and unexpected finding, not seen on the preoperative TTE, highlighting the superior visualization of TEE. The size (6x5mm) and mobility are concerning for potential embolic events.

<step 5> **Interpret Histopathology:**
    * **Histopathology of Resected Mass:** The mass is confirmed to be a papillary fibroelastoma (PFE). This is a benign primary cardiac tumor, but its location on the aortic valve and mobility make it clinically significant due to the risk of embolization.

<step 6> **Synthesize Findings and Formulate Final Diagnoses:**
The patient's clinical picture is dominated by severe coronary artery disease (triple vessel disease) leading to angina and a previous NSTEMI. This is well-supported by the history, risk factors, and coronary angiogram findings.  Independently, the intraoperative TEE and subsequent histopathology reveal a papillary fibroelastoma of the aortic valve.

Therefore, the patient has two distinct diagnoses:
    1. **Severe Coronary Artery Disease (Triple Vessel Disease) with prior Non-ST-segment Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI) and Class III Angina.** This explains the presenting symptoms and is the primary underlying cardiac condition.
    2. **Papillary Fibroelastoma (PFE) of the Aortic Valve (Non-Coronary Cusp).** This is an incidental finding during surgery, but a clinically relevant diagnosis due to the potential for embolization.

Both diagnoses are important and need to be included in the final diagnosis. The angina is likely due to the coronary artery disease, and the PFE is an additional, though benign, cardiac lesion found incidentally.

### Answer:
Final Diagnosis: Severe Coronary Artery Disease (Triple Vessel Disease) with prior Non-ST-segment Myocardial Infarction (NSTEMI) and Class III Angina; Papillary Fibroelastoma (PFE) of the Aortic Valve (Non-Coronary Cusp)
